subtitle: Microbes Must Have Parents!
summary: After Leeuwenhoek's death in 1723, a new microbe hunter was born in Italy: Lazzaro Spallanzani. A curious and determined boy, he fought his father's wish that he study law, secretly studying science until a mentor convinced his father to let him pursue research. Spallanzani became a priest and professor, thriving in an era when science was becoming fashionable, and turned his attention to the little animals Leeuwenhoek had discovered — just as a fierce debate raged over whether life could arise spontaneously from non-living matter.

segments.anchor_sentence: "Leeuwenhoek is dead, it is too bad, it is a loss that cannot be made good."

visual.scene_description: Young Spallanzani as a boy in the Italian countryside near Scandiano, pulling legs off beetles and bugs, examining them with intense curiosity while other children play in the background.
visual.animation_prompt: Simple linocut print style illustration, bold black outlines, flat muted colors in dusty rose and sage green and ochre, rough textured edges, hand-printed look on natural paper, children's book illustration, stocky Italian boy in 18th-century peasant clothing kneeling in sunlit woods near Scandiano, examining a beetle held close to his face with intense concentration, scattered insect parts on the ground beside him, foaming natural fountain visible through the trees behind, dappled light filtering through oak canopy

segments.anchor_sentence: Lazzaro was a dutiful son—and a politician of a son—so he didn't argue with his father or the priest.

visual.scene_description: Young Spallanzani secretly studies science books by candlelight while legal documents sit ignored on his desk, then visits the scientist Vallisnieri who champions his cause to his father.
visual.animation_prompt: Simple linocut print style illustration, bold black outlines, flat muted colors in dusty rose and sage green and ochre, rough textured edges, hand-printed look on natural paper, children's book illustration, young burly Italian man in 18th-century coat standing before an older professor Vallisnieri in a wood-paneled study filled with scientific instruments and specimen jars, the professor gesturing emphatically while pointing at open books of mathematics and natural philosophy on a heavy oak desk, tall arched window casting afternoon light across scattered papers

segments.anchor_sentence: At this time it was much more respectable and safe to be a scientist than it had been when Leeuwenhoek began his first grinding of lenses.

visual.scene_description: Spallanzani as a young priest and professor lecturing to wide-eyed university students, skipping stones on water in a scientific experiment, surrounded by the energy of 18th-century Enlightenment learning.
visual.animation_prompt: Simple linocut print style illustration, bold black outlines, flat muted colors in dusty rose and sage green and ochre, rough textured edges, hand-printed look on natural paper, children's book illustration, tall thin sharp-featured man in black Catholic priest cassock standing at edge of a lake skipping a flat stone across the water surface, concentric ripples spreading where stone bounces, open notebook with geometric diagrams tucked under his arm, 18th-century Italian university buildings visible on a distant hill, clear sky above

segments.anchor_sentence: The little animals had got themselves involved in a strange question, in a furious fight, and had it not been for that, they might have remained curiosities for centuries, or even have been completely forgotten.

visual.scene_description: A split scene showing the absurd old belief in spontaneous generation: a buried bullock with horns sticking out of the ground and bees flying out, contrasted with a circular microscope inset showing tiny wriggling microbes.
visual.animation_prompt: Simple linocut print style illustration, bold black outlines, flat muted colors in dusty rose and sage green and ochre, rough textured edges, hand-printed look on natural paper, children's book illustration, pair of curved bullock horns protruding from bare earth mound with a swarm of detailed bees buzzing upward from the horns, circular magnified inset in upper corner showing rod-shaped bacilli and round cocci swimming among each other with visible cell walls and flagella, 18th-century Italian countryside with cypress trees in background
